Count navigable 4-subsets in a 9-vertex circulant with 9 two-way edges

Let the vertex set be V = {0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8} (arithmetic mod 9). Define a directed graph G on V by the rule:
- For each i, the pair {i,i+1} is two-way: both edges i\toi+1 and i+1\toi are present.
- For all other pairs i\neqj, exactly one directed edge is present, given by:
    i\toj  iff  (j-i) mod 9 \in {2,3,4}.
Equivalently, every unordered pair has at least one direction, and the only two-way pairs are the 9 consecutive pairs.

Let M be the 9×9 adjacency matrix of G (M_ij = 1 if i\toj, else 0, and M has zero diagonal).
For any 4-subset S ⊂ V, let M_S be the 4×4 principal submatrix of M indexed by S.

Call S "navigable" if every off-diagonal entry of
  M_S + M_S^2 + M_S^3
is positive.

How many navigable 4-subsets S are there?

Solution.
Step 1 (interpret_navigable_as_reachability + adjacency_matrix_walk_count)
For a directed graph with adjacency matrix A, the entry (A^t)_{ij} counts the number of directed walks of length t from i to j.
Hence, for i\neqj in S,
  (M_S + M_S^2 + M_S^3)_{ij} > 0
iff there exists a directed walk from i to j of length 1, 2, or 3 using only vertices in S.

Therefore, S is navigable iff for every ordered pair of distinct vertices (i,j) in S, j is reachable from i by a walk of length \leq3 inside S.

Step 2 (reduce_to_strong_connectivity for k=4, L=3)
Any directed graph on 4 vertices, if strongly connected, has directed diameter at most 3 (since any shortest path between two vertices can be taken simple and uses at most 4 vertices, hence length \leq3).
So on |S|=4,
  "all pairs reachable within length \leq3"  ⇔  "the induced digraph on S is strongly connected."

Thus, counting navigable 4-subsets is the same as counting strongly connected induced subgraphs on 4 vertices.

Step 3 (count_bad_sets_by_source_component + center_locus_casework replaced by SCC-size casework)
Let S be a 4-subset whose induced digraph is NOT strongly connected. Consider the condensation DAG of strongly connected components (SCCs). It has a source SCC U (no edges into U from other SCCs).

We analyze by |U|.

Precompute local edge structure in G:
For any vertex v, the only two-way neighbors are v±1.
Also, the one-way out-neighbors of v are exactly v+2, v+3, v+4.
(The vertices v+5,v+6,v+7 are one-way in-neighbors of v.)

Case 3a: |U| = 1 (a source vertex)
Then U={v} where no other vertex in S has an edge into v.
In particular, no other vertex of S can be v±1, because v±1 \to v is present (two-way adjacency would create an incoming edge).
So the other three vertices are not adjacent to v.

Since every unordered pair has at least one directed edge, and no vertex in S\{v} can send an edge into v, it follows that v must send a (one-way) edge to each of the other three vertices.
But v has one-way edges only to v+2, v+3, v+4. Therefore
  S = {v, v+2, v+3, v+4}.                                  (Type A)

Such an S is indeed not strongly connected because v has indegree 0 within S.

Case 3b: |U| = 3 (equivalently, there is a sink vertex)
If the source SCC has size 3, then the remaining vertex w forms a sink SCC of size 1 (no edges leaving w to the other three SCC-vertices).
So w has outdegree 0 within S.

As above, w cannot be adjacent to any other vertex in S, because adjacency would give w\to(w±1) as well.
Thus the other three vertices must be exactly the three one-way in-neighbors of w, namely w-2, w-3, w-4.
Equivalently,
  S = {w-4, w-3, w-2, w} = {i, i+1, i+2, i+4} with i=w-4.   (Type B)

Such an S is not strongly connected because w cannot reach any other vertex in S.

Case 3c: |U| = 2 (a source SCC of size 2)
If U has size 2, then U must be strongly connected as a subgraph on two vertices. That is possible only if the two vertices have edges both ways between them, i.e., they are consecutive.
So U = {v, v+1}.

Because U is a source SCC, there are no edges from the other two vertices (call them x,y) into either v or v+1.
In particular, x and y cannot be adjacent to v or v+1 (otherwise they would have incoming edges into U via the two-way neighbor edge).
Thus each of x,y must lie in the one-way out-neighborhood of both v and v+1.

One-way out-neighborhoods:
  Out1(v)   = {v+2, v+3, v+4},
  Out1(v+1) = {v+3, v+4, v+5}.
Their intersection is {v+3, v+4}. Hence {x,y} must equal {v+3, v+4}, and therefore
  S = {v, v+1, v+3, v+4}.                                   (Type C)

Such an S is not strongly connected because every cross-edge from {v,v+1} to {v+3,v+4} goes forward, and there is no path back.

Conclusion of Step 3:
Every non-strongly-connected 4-set S must be of exactly one of the three forms:
(Type A) {v, v+2, v+3, v+4}
(Type B) {v, v+1, v+2, v+4}
(Type C) {v, v+1, v+3, v+4}
with v \in ℤ/9ℤ.

These three types are disjoint (they have distinct gap patterns around the 9-cycle), so we may count them separately.

Step 4 (symmetry_grouping_of_cases + finite_count)
For each fixed v, each of the sets in Types A, B, C is a well-defined 4-subset of V.
As v ranges over 9 values, we obtain 9 subsets of each type. Thus
  #bad 4-subsets = 9 + 9 + 9 = 27.

Total 4-subsets of V:
  C(9,4) = 126.

Therefore the number of navigable (strongly connected) 4-subsets is
  126 - 27 = 99.

Answer: 99.

Uniqueness Test
(Independence from the main flow; uses the uniqueness certificates in the metadata.)

1) Determinism of the navigable predicate:
For each 4-subset S, the matrix M_S is uniquely determined by the fixed construction of G, and the condition
  offdiag(M_S + M_S^2 + M_S^3) > 0
is a deterministic yes/no statement. Hence the number of navigable sets is well-defined and unique once we characterize which S fail.

2) Exhaustive classification of non-navigable sets:
If S is not navigable, then by Step 2 it is not strongly connected. The condensation DAG has a source SCC U with |U|\in{1,2,3}. Because the only two-way edges in G are between consecutive vertices, any SCC of size 2 must be a consecutive pair; and the "no incoming edges into U" requirement forces the remaining vertices to lie in specific one-way out-neighborhood intersections. This forces S into exactly one of Types A, B, C, and no other shapes are possible. Thus the set of bad 4-subsets is uniquely determined.

3) Disjointness and counting:
Types A, B, C are disjoint and each is parametrized by v\inℤ/9ℤ, giving exactly 9 subsets per type. Therefore the number of bad sets is uniquely 27, and the number of navigable sets is uniquely
  C(9,4) - 27 = 99.

99
